  • Download Rmd

The Sampling Distribution of the Mean

Alyssah

June 10, 2026

1. The Population

We start with a skewed population — drawn from an exponential distribution. This is intentional: an exponential distribution looks nothing like a normal curve. Using a non-normal population lets us clearly observe the Central Limit Theorem at work later on.

set.seed(42)

POPULATION_SIZE <- 100000
population <- rexp(POPULATION_SIZE, rate = 0.5)

pop_mean <- mean(population)
pop_sd   <- sd(population)

ggplot(data.frame(x = population), aes(x = x)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 80, fill = "#6B7280", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pop_mean, color = "#d7191c", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text",
           x = pop_mean + 1, y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = paste0("Population mean = ", round(pop_mean, 2)),
           color = "#d7191c", size = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Our Population",
    subtitle = paste0("Exponential distribution  |  N = ", format(POPULATION_SIZE, big.mark = ","),
                      "  |  μ = ", round(pop_mean, 2),
                      "  |  σ = ", round(pop_sd, 2)),
    x = "Value", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

What you should notice: The population is heavily right-skewed. Most values are near zero but a long tail stretches to the right. This is a realistic shape — income distributions, wait times, and many natural phenomena look like this.

2. What Is a Sampling Distribution?

If you draw a random sample of size n from this population and compute the sample mean, you get one number. Do that 1,000 times and you have 1,000 sample means. The distribution of those means is called the sampling distribution of the mean.

The key question: what does that distribution look like, and how does sample size change it?

We’ll answer this by running the experiment at three sample sizes:

Label Sample size n
Small 5
Medium 30
Large 100

3. Simulating 1,000 Samples

N_SAMPLES   <- 1000
small_n     <- 5
medium_n    <- 30
large_n     <- 100

sample_means <- function(pop, n, reps) {
  replicate(reps, mean(sample(pop, size = n, replace = FALSE)))
}

means_small  <- sample_means(population, small_n,  N_SAMPLES)
means_medium <- sample_means(population, medium_n, N_SAMPLES)
means_large  <- sample_means(population, large_n,  N_SAMPLES)

results <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(sample_mean = means_small,  size = paste0("Small (n = ",  small_n,  ")")),
  data.frame(sample_mean = means_medium, size = paste0("Medium (n = ", medium_n, ")")),
  data.frame(sample_mean = means_large,  size = paste0("Large (n = ",  large_n,  ")"))
) %>%
  mutate(size = factor(size, levels = c(
    paste0("Small (n = ",  small_n,  ")"),
    paste0("Medium (n = ", medium_n, ")"),
    paste0("Large (n = ",  large_n,  ")")
  )))

We now have a data frame with 3,000 rows: 1,000 sample means for each of the three sample sizes.

4. Visualising the Three Sampling Distributions

panel_colors <- setNames(
  c("#E07A5F", "#F4A261", "#2A9D8F"),
  c(paste0("Small (n = ",  small_n,  ")"),
    paste0("Medium (n = ", medium_n, ")"),
    paste0("Large (n = ",  large_n,  ")"))
)

ggplot(results, aes(x = sample_mean, fill = size)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 40, color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  stat_density(geom = "line", color = "black", linewidth = 0.7, adjust = 1.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pop_mean,
             color = "#d7191c", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~size, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = panel_colors, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Sampling Distribution of the Mean — 1,000 Simulations",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line = true population mean",
    x = "Sample Mean", y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12))

5. Overlaid Comparison

Placing all three distributions on one plot makes the shape and spread differences unmistakable.

ggplot(results, aes(x = sample_mean, color = size, fill = size)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.20, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pop_mean,
             color = "#d7191c", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = panel_colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = panel_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Overlaid Sampling Distributions",
    subtitle = "Larger samples → narrower, taller, more normal-shaped distributions",
    x = "Sample Mean", y = "Density",
    color = "Sample size", fill = "Sample size"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

What you should notice:

  • All three distributions are centred on the true population mean — sampling is unbiased regardless of n.
  • The small-sample distribution is wide and slightly skewed (it still carries echoes of the population’s shape).
  • The medium and large distributions look increasingly bell-shaped, even though the underlying population is exponential.
  • The large-sample distribution is tightly concentrated — large samples give much more precise estimates.

6. The Central Limit Theorem in Numbers

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) tells us exactly what to expect. For samples of size n drawn from a population with mean μ and standard deviation σ:

\[\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty\]

The quantity σ/√n is called the standard error of the mean (SE). Let’s compare the theoretical SE to what we actually observed in the simulation.

summary_tbl <- results %>%
  group_by(size) %>%
  summarise(
    n          = as.integer(sub(".*= (\\d+)\\)", "\\1", as.character(size[1]))),
    observed_mean = round(mean(sample_mean), 4),
    observed_se   = round(sd(sample_mean),   4),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    theoretical_se = round(pop_sd / sqrt(n), 4),
    ratio          = round(observed_se / theoretical_se, 3)
  )

knitr::kable(summary_tbl,
  col.names = c("Sample size", "n", "Observed mean", "Observed SE",
                "Theoretical SE (σ/√n)", "Ratio (obs/theory)"),
  caption   = "Comparison of observed simulation results vs. CLT predictions"
)
Comparison of observed simulation results vs. CLT predictions
Sample size n Observed mean Observed SE Theoretical SE (σ/√n) Ratio (obs/theory)
Small (n = 5) 5 2.0312 0.9348 0.8950 1.044
Medium (n = 30) 30 1.9811 0.3563 0.3654 0.975
Large (n = 100) 100 2.0001 0.2009 0.2001 1.004

The ratio column should be very close to 1.0, confirming the CLT prediction.

7. How Standard Error Shrinks with Sample Size

ns     <- 1:200
se_df  <- data.frame(n = ns, se = pop_sd / sqrt(ns))
vlines <- data.frame(n = c(small_n, medium_n, large_n),
                     label = c("small", "medium", "large"),
                     color = c("#E07A5F", "#F4A261", "#2A9D8F"))

ggplot(se_df, aes(x = n, y = se)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#5C4033") +
  geom_vline(data = vlines, aes(xintercept = n, color = label),
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(data = vlines, aes(x = n, y = pop_sd / sqrt(n), color = label),
             size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = setNames(vlines$color, vlines$label)) +
  labs(
    title = "Standard Error vs. Sample Size",
    subtitle = "SE = σ / √n  —  diminishing returns beyond ~50",
    x = "Sample size (n)", y = "Standard Error",
    color = "Our samples"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Key insight: Going from n = 5 to n = 30 yields a dramatic drop in SE. Going from n = 30 to n = 100 helps, but the gains are smaller. Doubling the sample size cuts SE by a factor of √2 ≈ 1.41 — not 2. This is the law of diminishing returns in sampling.

8. Summary

Concept What we saw
Unbiasedness All three sampling distributions centred on the true population mean μ.
Central Limit Theorem Even from a skewed exponential population, sample means became approximately normal as n grew.
Standard error SE = σ/√n — observed SEs matched the formula closely.
Diminishing returns Halving SE requires quadrupling n.

The sampling distribution of the mean is the foundation of confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and almost every inferential statistic you will encounter. Understanding that the sample mean is itself a random variable — with its own distribution, mean, and spread — is the key conceptual leap in statistics.
